1: presbyterous tous en hymin parakalō ho sympresbyteros kai martys ton tou Christou pathēmatōn: 'To the elders among you, I appeal as a fellow elder and a witness of Christ's sufferings.' Sympresbyteros (fellow elder) is a NT hapax — Peter places himself alongside rather than above the elders he addresses. Martys tōn tou Christou pathēmatōn (witness of Christ's sufferings) — martys is the legal term for a courtroom witness whose testimony is based on direct observation; Peter's witness grounds his authority in eyewitness proximity to the passion. The witness-language anticipates the later technical sense of martys as martyr (one who witnesses to Christ at the cost of life).
2: poimanate to en hymin poimnion tou theou episkopountes mē anankastōs alla hekousiōs kata theon mēde aischrokerdōs alla prothumōs: 'Shepherd the flock of God that is among you, exercising oversight — not under compulsion, but willingly, as God would have you; not for shameful gain, but eagerly.' Poimanate (shepherd) is the imperative of poimainō — the same word Jesus uses in John 21:16 ('tend my sheep') in his post-resurrection commission to Peter. Episkopountes (exercising oversight) — the elder/shepherd/episkopos overlap is explicit: the three office-terms are functionally synonymous in the Pastoral and General Epistles (cf. Acts 20:28; Titus 1:5-7). The three contrasting pairs frame Christian leadership as voluntary, non-commercial, and non-coercive.
3: mēde hōs katakyrienontes tōn klērōn all' typoi ginomenoi tou poimniou: 'not domineering over those in your charge, but being examples to the flock.' Katakyrienontes (domineering, lording it over) — the identical compound Jesus uses in Mark 10:42 ('those who are considered rulers lord it over them') to contrast worldly power with servant-leadership. Klērōn — klēros is the lot or allotted portion; the flock-members are God's property assigned to the elder, not the elder's own possession. Typos (example, pattern, type) — the elder's authority is embodied demonstration, not institutional power; the community is shaped by what they see in the leader, not merely by what they are commanded to do.
4: kai phanerōthentos tou archipoimenos komisesthe ton amarantinon tēs doxēs stephanon: 'And when the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of unfading glory.' Archipoimēn (Chief Shepherd) — a NT hapax legomenon, found nowhere in prior Greek literature; it places all local elders as sub-shepherds under the supreme Shepherd whose appearing is the parousia. Amarantinon (unfading, from amarantos, the flower that does not wither) — contrasted with the perishable laurel or celery wreaths of Greek athletic victories, which wilted within days. The crown of glory is the eschatological reward at the appearing (phanerōthentos, aorist passive participle of phaneroō, the epiphany-word) of the Chief Shepherd.
5: pantes de allēlois tēn tapeinophrosynēn enkombōsasthe — enkombōsasthe (clothe yourselves with, tie on) is the verb for tying on a garment by knotting it; specifically associated with tying on the encomboma, the apron or loincloth of a slave's work-garment. The word appears to echo Jesus's action at the Last Supper (John 13:4-5: 'he laid aside his outer garments... and tied a towel around himself'). The quotation of Prov 3:34 LXX (ho theos hyperēphanois antitassetai tapeinois de didōsin charin) is verbatim with James 4:6, indicating this was a widely circulated community warrant for humility under pressure. Tapeinophrosynē (humility, lowly-mindedness) — a compound the Greek world regarded as servile and shameful; the NT rehabilitates it as the primary virtue of the community that follows the humble Servant.
6: tapeinōthēte oun hypo tēn krataian cheira tou theou hina hymas hypsōsē en kairō: 'Humble yourselves, therefore, under the mighty hand of God, so that at the proper time he may exalt you.' Krataia cheir tou theou (mighty hand of God) — the Exodus formula (Deut 3:24; Exod 6:1; 1 Kgs 8:42 LXX): the same hand that rescued Israel from Egypt is the hand under which the community submits in the present. Submission to this hand is not oppression but the posture that positions for eschatological lifting-up. En kairō (at the proper time) — kairos is the divinely appointed moment, not clock-time; the exaltation is certain but the timing belongs to God's sovereign calendar.
7: pasan tēn merimnan hymōn epiripsontes ep' auton hoti autō melei peri hymōn: 'casting all your anxieties on him, because he cares for you.' Epiripsontes (casting, throwing upon) — the aorist participle of epiriptō, the same word in LXX Ps 55:22: 'Cast your burden on the LORD, and he will sustain you.' Grammatically the participle is dependent on tapeinōthēte (v. 6) — the means of humbling oneself under God's hand is the active transfer of anxiety to him. Melei autō peri hymōn (it matters to him concerning you) — the verb melō expresses personal caring interest; God is not an indifferent sovereign but a shepherd who attends to the flock's welfare.
8: nēpsate grēgorēsate ho antidikos hymōn diabolos hōs leōn ōryomenos peripatei zētōn tina katapiein: 'Be sober-minded, be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour.' Nēpsate, grēgorēsate (be sober, be watchful) — the standard NT pair for spiritual vigilance (1 Thess 5:6-8; Mark 13:33-37). Antidikos (adversary) — the legal term for the opposing party in a lawsuit; the diabolos as accuser in the heavenly court (Job 1-2; Zech 3:1; Rev 12:10: 'the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down'). Leōn ōryomenos (roaring lion) — draws on Ps 22:13 ('they open wide their mouths at me, like a ravening and roaring lion') and Ps 91:13 ('you will tread on the lion and the cobra').
9: hō antistēte stereoi tē pistei eidotes ta auta tōn pathēmatōn tē en tō kosmō hymōn adelphotēti epiteleisthai: 'Resist him, firm in your faith, knowing that the same kinds of suffering are being experienced by your brotherhood throughout the world.' Antistēte (resist) — the same imperative as James 4:7; the shared use of Prov 3:34 LXX and this devil-resistance command suggests a common tradition circulating in the General Epistles community. Stereoi (firm, solid) — architectural language for load-bearing masonry; faith as the structural member that sustains pressure without collapsing. Adelphotēs (brotherhood) — a NT rare word (only here and 1 Pet 2:17); it names the universal community of believers as a single kinship-network spread across the Roman Empire's geography but unified in suffering and faith.
10: ho de theos pasēs charitos ho kalesas hymas eis tēn aiōnion autou doxan en Christō oligon pathontas autos katartisai hymas stērixai sthenōsai themeliosei: 'And the God of all grace... will himself restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish you.' Ho theos pasēs charitos (the God of all grace) — a unique divine title in the NT; pasēs charitos (of all grace) declares that every variety and quantity of grace has a single divine source. Oligon (a little while) — the temporal brevity of suffering placed against eternal glory (cf. 2 Cor 4:17). The four aorist infinitives express God's own restorative action: katartisai (restore/equip — the word for mending torn fishing nets, Mark 1:19, and restoring a fallen person, Gal 6:1), stērixai (establish), sthenōsai (strengthen), themeliosei (lay a foundation under) — each is a building or structural term for God's post-suffering reconstruction.
11: autō to kratos eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn amēn: 'To him be the dominion forever and ever. Amen.' Kratos (dominion, ruling power) is the doxological term for God's sovereign authority: Rev 1:6 ('to him be glory and dominion forever'), Jude 25, 1 Tim 6:16. The brief doxology closes the letter's body before the epistolary conclusion of vv. 12-14. Its brevity concentrates all the letter's theology into the simple declaration that the God of grace (v. 10) who rescues and restores is the one who holds sovereign power — a counter-claim against imperial pretensions to divine dominion.
12: dia Silouanou hymin tou pistou adelphou hōs logizomai di' oligōn egraxa parakalōn kai epimarturōn tautēn einai alēthē charin tou theou eis hēn stēte: 'Through Silvanus... I have written briefly, encouraging you and testifying that this is the true grace of God. Stand firm in it.' Silouanos (Silvanus/Silas) — Paul's co-worker (2 Thess 1:1; 1 Thess 1:1; Acts 15:22, 40), whose association with both Peter and Paul bridges the Petrine and Pauline traditions. Di' Silouanou egraxa (through Silvanus I have written) — dia with the genitive may mean he was amanuensis (took dictation), scribe who drafted in polished Greek from Peter's Aramaic, or letter-carrier. Epimarturōn (testifying, bearing witness to) returns the letter to its opening witness-language (v. 1): the same apostolic authority grounds both personal appeal and doctrinal affirmation.
13: aspazetai hymas hē en Babylōni syneklektē kai Markos ho huios mou: 'She who is in Babylon, chosen together with you, sends you greetings, and so does my son Mark.' Hē en Babylōni — the personified local church at Peter's location; in Christian apocalyptic, Babylon = Rome (Rev 14:8; 17:5; 18:2; 4 Ezra 3:1-2 where Babylon = Rome in Jewish apocalyptic contemporary with 1 Peter). The code-name functions as both discretion under Roman persecution and theological commentary: Rome is Babylon, the empire of captivity from which God delivers. Markos ho huios mou (Mark my son) — Papias (in Eusebius HE 3.39) records that Mark composed his Gospel from Peter's preaching: 'Mark, having become the interpreter of Peter, wrote down accurately everything he remembered of the things said and done by the Lord.'
14: aspasasthe allēlous en philēmati agapēs eirēnē pasin tois en Christō: 'Greet one another with a kiss of love. Peace to all of you who are in Christ.' Philēmati agapēs (kiss of love) — the liturgical community greeting established in the Pauline letters (Rom 16:16; 1 Cor 16:20; 2 Cor 13:12; 1 Thess 5:26) and later formalized in the eucharistic liturgy (Justin Martyr, Apology 1.65, ca. 155 CE). The agapēs qualifier distinguishes it from ordinary social greetings — it is the enacted expression of the community's distinctive love for one another. En Christō (in Christ) — the final incorporative formula: peace is not merely wished but located; the community exists in Christ as its social and spiritual space, and peace characterizes that shared location.
